statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
Muhammadali has an integer array $ a_1,\dots,a_n $ . He can change (replace) any subset of positions; changing position $ i $ costs $ c_i $ and replaces $ a_i $ with any integer of his choice. The positions that he does not change must retain their original values.

After all changes, we call an index $ i $ ( $ 1 \le i < n $ ) a drop if the final value at position $ i $ is strictly greater than the final value at position $ i+1 $ . Muhammadali wants the final array to contain no drops.

Find the minimum cost of changes required to ensure that there are no drops in the array.

Input Format
The first line contains an integer $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 5000 $ ) — the number of test cases.

Each test case consists of three lines:

The first line contains a single integer $ n $ ( $ 1 \le n \le 8000 $ ) — the length of the arrays.

The second line contains $ n $ integers $ a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n $ ( $ 1 \le a_i \le 10^9 $ ) — the elements of the array.

The third line contains $ n $ integers $ c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n $ ( $ 1 \le c_i \le 10^9 $ ) — the costs of changes.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 8000 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer — the minimum possible total cost required to eliminate all drops.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
10
1
10
5
4
1 2 2 3
5 6 7 8
4
4 3 2 1
1 1 1 1
3
3 1 2
100 1 1
5
5 5 5 5 5
10 1 10 1 10
5
1 3 2 2 4
100 1 1 1 100
6
10 9 8 7 6 5
1 100 1 100 1 100
5
100 1 100 100 100
1 100 1 1 1
4
2 1 2 1
5 4 3 2
7
1 5 3 4 2 6 7
10 1 10 1 10 1 10
Output
0
0
3
2
0
1
203
1
6
11

Note
In the first and second examples, the array already has no drops, so no changes are needed.

In the third example, one of the optimal arrays is: $ [2,3,5,6] $ ; to achieve this, all elements except the second need to be replaced, so the answer is $ c_1 + c_3 + c_4 = 3 $ .

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content: In finding simple LNDS, all elements have the same "weights". But here we are given weights.

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2167G - Mukhammadali and the Smooth Array
If we decide to
keep
an index $$$i$$$, its value remains $$$a_i$$$. Hence, the kept indices must form a
non-decreasing subsequence
of $$$a$$$.
Our goal is to minimize the total cost. Equivalently, we can
maximize
the sum of $$$c_i$$$ over the kept indices, since
$$$$$$ cost = \sum c_i - \sum_{kept} c_i $$$$$$
Thus, the problem reduces to finding a non-decreasing subsequence that maximizes the sum of corresponding $$$c_i$$$.
Let $$$dp[i]$$$ be the maximum sum of $$$c$$$ in a non-decreasing subsequence that ends at index $$$i$$$.
For each $$$i$$$, we can append $$$a_i$$$ to any valid subsequence that ends at some $$$j  \lt  i$$$ with $$$a_j \le a_i$$$:
$$$$$$ dp[i] = c_i + \max_{j  \lt  i,\, a_j \le a_i} dp[j] $$$$$$
If there is no such $$$j$$$, we simply take $$$dp[i] = c_i$$$.
After computing all $$$dp[i]$$$, the maximum achievable sum of kept elements is $$$\max_i dp[i]$$$.
Hence, the final answer is
$$$$$$ answer = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i - \max_i dp[i] $$$$$$
